topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The solicitor’s pronouncement felt like a physical weight settling on my shoulders. "You and your brother have inherited the property." I stared at the deed, the elegant script mocking me with its inherited grandeur. This sprawling, dusty old house, Grandma Elara's legacy, was now ours. I let out a slow, deliberate breath, a sound that carried more judgment than actual relief. I surveyed the peeling wallpaper and the sagging porch, my eyes lingering on every imperfection as if cataloging flaws in a poorly assembled machine. My brother, Mark, was already bustling, his energy a jarring contrast to my own stillness. "Well," I drawled, my voice flat, "it's certainly... substantial." I turned toward the front door, already planning the most efficient way to vacate this suffocating inheritance.

[story 2]

I traced the intricate carvings on the mahogany banister with a fingertip, the wood cool and faintly tacky beneath my touch. The sheer volume of the estate overwhelmed me; it was too much history, too much sentimentality. I felt a distinct tightening in my chest, a sensation akin to being trapped in a poorly ventilated room. Mark, meanwhile, was already sketching plans on a notepad, his enthusiasm radiating off him like heat. "It’s magnificent, isn't it?" he chirped, oblivious to the palpable sourness emanating from me. I didn't look up. My gaze remained fixed on a cobweb draped across a stained-glass window. "It's certainly... large," I managed, the word clipped, tasting like ash on my tongue. I needed to find the exit, the quickest route away from this mausoleum of memory.

[story 3]

The air in the drawing-room was thick with the scent of aged lavender and neglect. I crossed my arms tightly across my chest, a defensive posture against the oppressive atmosphere. Grandma Elara’s house was a monument to things I actively avoided—faded elegance and suffocating tradition. I watched Mark attempt to charm a reluctant antique dealer, his practiced smile feeling entirely undeserved in this setting. I shifted my weight, my boots scraping lightly on the parquet floor, a small, sharp movement signaling my profound lack of interest. "I suppose," I said, my tone laced with an unmistakable lack of enthusiasm, "we'll have to sort through all this... junk." I didn't wait for a reply, already moving toward the library, seeking the quiet solitude where I could best observe the absurdity of the situation from a safe distance.
